tagline: Calculate posts, wire, gates, and cost for barbed wire, high-tensile, board, or electric fencing
title: Cattle Fencing Calculator — Posts, Wire, Gates & Cost Estimate
metaDescription: Calculate exactly how many posts, wire rolls, and gates you need for cattle fencing. Get material lists and cost estimates for barbed wire, high-tensile, board, and electric fence by acreage.

jsonLdWeb.name: Cattle Fencing Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate cattle fencing materials including posts, wire, gates, and cost estimates for barbed wire, high-tensile, board, and electric fencing.

mainEntity.name: How many fence posts do I need per acre?

acceptedAnswer.text: For a square acre (208.7 ft per side, 835 ft perimeter), you need about 105 t-posts at 8-foot spacing plus 4 corner posts and 1-2 gate posts. Post spacing varies: 8 ft for barbed wire, 10-12 ft for high-tensile, 8 ft for board fence.

mainEntity.name: How much does cattle fencing cost per foot?

acceptedAnswer.text: 4-strand barbed wire costs $1.50-$3.00/ft installed. High-tensile runs $1.00-$2.50/ft. Board fencing is the most expensive at $6-$12/ft. Electric fencing is cheapest at $0.50-$1.50/ft.

mainEntity.name: How many strands of barbed wire for cattle?

acceptedAnswer.text: Four strands is standard for cattle. The bottom wire should be 12 inches off the ground, with subsequent strands at 10-12 inch intervals. For bulls or aggressive cattle, 5 strands may be needed.

mainEntity.name: How far apart should fence posts be?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard spacing is 8-10 feet for barbed wire and board fencing. High-tensile fence can use 12-15 foot spacing with added stays. Electric fence uses 20-50 foot post spacing with step-in posts.

mainEntity.name: How many gates do I need for a cattle pasture?

acceptedAnswer.text: Plan at least one 12-16 foot gate per pasture for equipment access. Add walk-through gates at frequently used access points. For rotational grazing, include gates between each paddock section.

mainEntity.name: What is the cheapest cattle fencing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Electric fencing is the cheapest option at $0.50-$1.50 per foot. It requires an energizer ($100-$300) but uses fewer posts and less wire. High-tensile is the next most economical for permanent fencing.

howItWorks:
How the Cattle Fencing Calculator Works

This calculator estimates materials based on perimeter length and fence type, with costs at average material prices.

Perimeter (square) = 4 × √(Acres × 43,560)
  • Perimeter: Auto-calculated assuming square shape, or enter custom
  • Posts: Spaced per fence type (8-30 ft), plus corner and gate posts
  • Wire/rails: Total linear feet × number of strands or rails
  • Gates: Standard 12-ft livestock gates at user-specified count
  • Cost: Average material prices, labor not included
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How many fence posts do I need per acre?

For a square acre (835 ft perimeter), you need about 105 t-posts at 8-foot spacing plus 4 corner posts. High-tensile fencing uses fewer posts at 12-foot spacing, and electric uses the fewest at 30-foot spacing.

How much does cattle fencing cost per foot?

Barbed wire costs $1.50-$3.00 per foot for materials. High-tensile runs $1.00-$2.50/ft. Board fencing is $6-$12/ft. Electric is cheapest at $0.50-$1.50/ft. Add 50-100% for professional installation labor.

How many strands of barbed wire for cattle?

Four strands is standard for most cattle. Bottom wire at 12 inches high, each strand spaced 10-12 inches apart. For bulls or aggressive cattle, consider 5 strands or high-tensile alternatives.

How far apart should fence posts be?

Barbed wire and board fences use 8-foot spacing. High-tensile can stretch to 12-15 feet with stays between posts. Electric fencing allows 20-50 foot spacing depending on terrain and wire type.

How many gates do I need?

At minimum, one 12-16 foot equipment gate per pasture. Add walk-through gates at frequent access points. For rotational grazing, plan a gate between each paddock. Most small pastures use 2-3 gates total.

What is the cheapest cattle fencing?

Electric fencing is cheapest upfront at around $0.50-$1.50 per foot plus an energizer. For permanent fencing, high-tensile offers the best long-term value with a 20-30 year lifespan and lower per-foot cost than barbed wire.

howWeCalculate.intro: The math behind cattle fencing is geometry plus a materials multiplier. Perimeter sets how much fence line you have to build; spacing and strand count turn that line into a post count and a wire order. Here is exactly what happens when you press Calculate.
steps: Perimeter is found from acreage assuming a square field: side = √(acres × 43,560), then perimeter = 4 × side. A square 40-acre pasture works out to about 5,280 ft (a full mile). Enter a custom perimeter if your field is a long rectangle or an odd shape — the same acreage on a 4:1 rectangle can add 25% more fence line.
Line posts = perimeter ÷ spacing (8 ft for barbed and board, 12 ft for high-tensile, 30 ft for electric), then 4 corner posts and a post on each side of every gate are added on top.

Wire is perimeter × number of strands (4 for standard barbed, 5-6 for high-tensile). That total footage is divided by 1,320 ft — the length of one quarter-mile roll — to get the number of rolls to order. Board fence multiplies perimeter by 3 rails instead.
Cost multiplies total fence footage by the per-foot material price for the chosen fence type and adds gate hardware, giving a materials-only estimate.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These are planning estimates for a square field at average catalog prices, materials only. Rolling ground, brush clearing, rock, corner-brace assemblies, and installed labor (often 50-100% on top of materials) are not included. Walk and measure your actual fence line before buying, and add 5-10% for waste and splices.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this calculator when you are turning a pasture into a purchase order and need a defensible material count before you call the co-op or a fencing contractor.
scenarios: Pricing a new perimeter fence for a pasture you just bought or leased, straight from the deed acreage
Building a materials shopping list — corner posts, t-posts, rolls of barbed wire, and gates — before a trip to the farm store
Comparing barbed wire against high-tensile or electric on the same acreage to see which fits the budget
Setting up rotational-grazing paddocks and needing a wire and post count for each cross-fence
Sanity-checking a contractor's bid so you know whether the post count and wire footage look right

sections.title: Turn Acreage Into a Cattle Fence Material List
sections.body: Feed this calculator your pasture size and it hands back the four numbers that go on a fencing order: how many corner and line posts to set, how much wire to buy, how many quarter-mile rolls that is, and how many gates you asked for. It handles 4-strand barbed wire, high-tensile, 3-rail board fence, and electric, because each one changes the post spacing and the strand count — and therefore the price. Start from acreage for a square field, or type in a measured perimeter when your ground is a long rectangle.

sections.title: A Worked Example: Fencing a 40-Acre Pasture
sections.body: Say you are fencing a square 40-acre pasture. Perimeter comes to roughly 5,280 ft — a full mile. At 20 ft line-post spacing that is about 264 line posts, plus 4 corner posts and a pair on each side of your gates. A 5-strand barbed fence needs 5 × 5,280 = 26,400 ft of wire, which at 1,320 ft per quarter-mile roll is about 20 rolls. Tighten spacing to 16 ft for rougher ground and the post count climbs to around 330; drop to a 4-strand fence and you save four rolls of wire. Change any input and the material list updates so you can see the trade-off before you spend.

sections.title: Post Spacing, Strand Count, and Where the Money Goes
sections.body: Line posts typically sit 15-20 ft apart for barbed cattle fence, closer on hills and washes, wider on flat ground. Four strands is the working standard for cattle, with the bottom wire about 12 inches up and each strand 10-12 inches above the last; 5 strands is common for bulls or where you need to hold calves. The real budget swing is fence type: electric wire is cheap per foot but needs an energizer and grounding, high-tensile costs more upfront but lasts 20-30 years, and board fence is several times the price of wire. Corners and gate braces are where a fence lives or dies, so set 5-6 inch wood posts deep and brace them properly.

howToSteps.title: Enter the Acreage You're Fencing
howToSteps.description: Type in the pasture size in acres. For a square field the calculator turns that into a perimeter automatically — 40 acres becomes about 5,280 ft of fence line.

howToSteps.title: Set Perimeter, Fence Type, and Gates
howToSteps.description: Override the perimeter if your field is a long rectangle or irregular shape. Pick barbed wire, high-tensile, board, or electric — each changes post spacing and strand count — then enter how many gates you need.

howToSteps.title: Calculate the Material List
howToSteps.description: Press Calculate to get corner posts, line posts, wire rolls (or board rails), gates, and a materials cost estimate, all figured instantly in your browser.

howToSteps.title: Review and Adjust
howToSteps.description: Check the post count and roll count against your ground. Tighten spacing for hilly terrain, compare fence types side by side, and add 5-10% for waste before you order.

tips: Line posts every 15-20 ft suit flat ground; drop to 12-16 ft over hills, dips, and washes so the wire follows the contour instead of floating.
Barbed cattle fence buys wire by the quarter-mile roll (1,320 ft). Multiply your perimeter by the number of strands, then divide by 1,320 to get rolls — round up.
Corners and gate posts are where a fence fails first. Set 5-6 inch wood posts 3-3.5 ft deep and build a proper H-brace or diagonal brace at every corner.
A long, skinny rectangle has far more perimeter than a square of the same acreage, so measure or map your real fence line rather than trusting the square estimate.
Add 5-10% extra wire and a handful of spare t-posts for splices, stretches, and the post you'll inevitably bend driving it.

faq.question: How far apart should I set fence posts for cattle?
faq.answer: For barbed cattle fence, line posts usually go 15-20 ft apart on flat ground, tightening to 12-16 ft over hills and washes. High-tensile can stretch to 12-15 ft with stays between posts, and electric fence runs 20-50 ft apart. Corner and gate posts are set separately as heavy braced wood posts.

faq.question: How many strands of barbed wire do cattle need?
faq.answer: Four strands is the standard for cattle, with the bottom wire about 12 inches off the ground and each strand 10-12 inches above the last. Bump up to 5 strands for bulls, for holding calves, or along roads and property lines where you want extra containment.

faq.question: How much wire do I need per acre for cattle fence?
faq.answer: It depends on shape, not just acreage, because wire tracks the perimeter. A square 40-acre pasture has about 5,280 ft of perimeter, so a 5-strand fence needs roughly 26,400 ft of wire — about 20 quarter-mile rolls, or roughly half a roll per acre. A long rectangle of the same acreage needs noticeably more.

faq.question: How many rolls of barbed wire is that?
faq.answer: Barbed wire is sold in quarter-mile rolls of 1,320 ft. Take your perimeter, multiply by the number of strands, and divide by 1,320. For 5 strands around a 5,280 ft perimeter that's 26,400 ÷ 1,320 = 20 rolls. Always round up and add a little for splices.

faq.question: How do I figure the perimeter if I only know the acreage?
faq.answer: For a square field, side length = √(acres × 43,560 sq ft) and perimeter = 4 × side. Forty acres gives about 5,280 ft. Real fields are rarely perfect squares, so this is a floor — walk or map the actual boundary, since a rectangle adds fence line fast.

faq.question: How many gates should a cattle pasture have?
faq.answer: Plan at least one 12-16 ft gate wide enough for a truck, trailer, or tractor. Add walk-through gates at spots you use often, and for rotational grazing put a gate between each paddock. Every gate also needs a braced post on both sides, which the calculator adds to your post count.
